title: Psalms 65
subtitle: Praise Awaits You, Our God, in Zion

heb.paragraph: "Praise (təhillâ) awaits you, our God, in Zion" (v.1). A harvest thanksgiving psalm. God hears prayer (v.2), atones for transgressions (v.3), satisfies with the good things of his house (v.4). He stills the roaring of seas and the turmoil of nations (v.7). Those at the ends of the earth stand in awe (v.8).

notes.note: "You who answer prayer, to you all people will come. When we were overwhelmed by sins, you forgave our transgressions. Blessed are those you choose and bring near." Prayer heard, sins forgiven, people chosen — three gifts in three verses.
"You answer us with awesome and righteous deeds. You still the roaring of the seas. The whole earth is filled with awe." God’s deeds fill the earth from Zion to the ends.

refs.note: Jesus's invitation to the weary parallels the psalm's blessing on those whom God chooses and brings near.
Being brought near by Christ's blood fulfills the psalm's temple-access theology.
Confidence to enter the Most Holy Place completes the psalm's vision of approaching God's courts.

hist.context: Psalm 65 is a thanksgiving hymn celebrating harvest, likely used in temple liturgy during agricultural festivals. The opening acknowledges that 'praise awaits' (or 'is fitting for') God in Zion. The blessing on those who 'dwell in your courts' (v.4) may refer to priests or pilgrims granted temple access. The psalm transitions from temple to cosmos, from forgiveness to fertility.

sections.header: Verses 9–13 — “The Grasslands of the Wilderness Overflow”

heb.paragraph: "You care for the land and water it; you enrich it abundantly. The streams (peleg) of God are filled with water to provide the people with grain" (v.9). The harvest poem: God waters, softens, levels, blesses. Carts overflow with abundance (v.11). Meadows are clothed with flocks, valleys mantled with grain — "they shout for joy and sing" (v.13). Nature worships.

notes.note: "You care for the land and water it abundantly. You soften it with showers and bless its crops." God is the farmer’s partner: every harvest is divine provision. Agriculture is theology.

"The grasslands of the wilderness overflow; the hills are clothed with gladness. The meadows are covered with flocks and the valleys with grain; they shout for joy and sing." Creation rejoices. Nature does not merely produce — it celebrates. The landscape sings.

refs.note: God providing rain and crops and filling hearts with joy echoes the psalm's creation theology.
The Father sending rain on righteous and unrighteous reflects the psalm's universal provision.
God's invisible qualities understood through creation parallels the psalm's natural revelation.

hist.context: The agricultural imagery (vv.9-13) describes the full cycle of rain, grain growth, and harvest. God 'crowns the year with bounty' (v.11) pictures a circular year wearing abundance like a garland. The hills 'clothed with gladness' (v.13) personifies creation rejoicing. This psalm was likely sung at Sukkot (Feast of Tabernacles) celebrating harvest completion.

ppl.name: God (Elohim)
ppl.role: Creator who waters the earth and crowns the year with abundance (vv.1-13)
ppl.text: This harvest thanksgiving moves from temple (vv.1-4) to cosmos (vv.5-8) to farmland (vv.9-13). God 'answers with awesome and righteous deeds' (v.5) and 'stills the roaring of the seas' (v.7) — creation's chaos yields to divine order. The agricultural imagery is lavish: God 'waters its furrows abundantly, settles its ridges, softens it with showers, and blesses its growth' (v.10). The year is 'crowned with bounty' and God's 'carts overflow with abundance' (v.11). Nature itself worships: 'the meadows are covered with flocks and the valleys are mantled with grain; they shout for joy and sing' (v.13).

ppl.name: David
ppl.role: Attributed author composing a creation-harvest hymn (superscription)
ppl.text: David's psalm moves from personal atonement ('you forgave the iniquity of our sins,' v.3) through cosmic power to agricultural blessing — an unusual theological arc linking forgiveness, creation, and harvest. 'Blessed are those you choose and bring near to live in your courts' (v.4) frames temple access as divine election, not human achievement. The psalm contains no petition — it is pure praise, making it one of the Psalter's most joyful compositions.

ppl.name: All Humanity
ppl.role: Universal recipients of God's awesome deeds (vv.2,5,8)
ppl.text: 'To you all people will come' (v.2) and God is 'the hope of all the ends of the earth and of the farthest seas' (v.5). The 'whole earth is filled with awe' at God's wonders (v.8). This universalism — God as provider for all creation, not Israel alone — anticipates the New Testament's extension of covenant blessings to all nations and grounds environmental theology in divine ownership and care.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVThe whole earth is filled with awe at your wonders; where morning dawns, where evening fades, you call forth songs of joy.ESVso that those who dwell at the ends of the earth are in awe at your signs. You make the going out of the morning and the evening to shout for joy.NIVYou care for the land and water it; you enrich it abundantly. The streams of God are filled with water to provide the people with grain, for so you have ordained it.ESVYou visit the earth and water it; you greatly enrich it; the river of God is full of water; you provide their grain, for so you have prepared it.

rec.text: ""You who answer prayer, to you all people will come. When we were overwhelmed by sins, you forgave our transgressions. Blessed are those you choose and bring near." Prayer heard, sins forgiven, people…"

rows.label: The Hearing God — 65:1–8
rows.text: "You who hear prayer." Sins forgiven. Seas stilled. The earth filled with awe.

hebtext.note: "Praise (təhillâ) awaits you, our God, in Zion" (v.1). A harvest thanksgiving psalm. God hears prayer (v.2), atones for transgressions (v.3), satisfies with the good things of his house (v.4). He stills the roaring of seas and the turmoil of nations (v.7). Those at the ends of the earth stand in awe (v.8).

"You care for the land and water it; you enrich it abundantly. The streams (peleg) of God are filled with water to provide the people with grain" (v.9). The harvest poem: God waters, softens, levels, blesses. Carts overflow with abundance (v.11). Meadows are clothed with flocks, valleys mantled with grain — "they shout for joy and sing" (v.13). Nature worships.

themes.note: Worship peaks: nature itself sings. Sovereignty: God waters the earth and stills the seas. Mercy: sins forgiven, prayer heard. The harvest psalm.

prayer_prompt: As you consider 'Praise awaits you, our God, in Zion,' consider asking God whether you see the abundance of the natural world — streams, grain, flocks — as evidence of His generosity rather than random good fortune.
